description: Comprehensive gazetteer of geographic locations mentioned in the Book of Genesis. Coordinates are WGS84 decimal degrees. Identifications follow standard archaeological and scholarly consensus (Anchor Bible Dictionary, Aharoni, Rainey, Tübingen Bible Atlas, etc.).

locations.name: Garden of Eden
locations.notes: Location entirely uncertain. Traditional placement near confluence of Tigris and Euphrates in southern Mesopotamia (near modern Qurna, Iraq). Some scholars propose Armenian highlands where Tigris and Euphrates originate. The Pishon and Gihon rivers are unidentified. Coordinates given are the southern Mesopotamia tradition.

locations.name: Pishon
locations.notes: One of four rivers of Eden. Said to compass the land of Havilah. Identification debated: proposed as Wadi Batin (dried river in Arabia), the Ganges (Josephus), or the Karun River in Iran. No scholarly consensus.

locations.name: Gihon
locations.notes: One of four rivers of Eden. Said to compass the land of Cush. Josephus identified it with the Nile; others suggest the Diyala or Karun rivers. Not to be confused with the Gihon Spring in Jerusalem. No scholarly consensus.

locations.name: Tigris
locations.notes: Major Mesopotamian river, well identified. Hebrew 'Hiddekel' from Akkadian 'Idiqlat'. Flows ~1,850 km from southeastern Turkey through Iraq to the Persian Gulf. Coordinates approximate midpoint near Baghdad.

locations.name: Euphrates
locations.notes: Major Mesopotamian river, well identified. Hebrew 'Perath' from Akkadian 'Purattu'. Longest river in western Asia (~2,800 km). Coordinates approximate midpoint near Ramadi, Iraq.

locations.name: Havilah
locations.notes: Region associated with the Pishon river and gold. Usually identified with part of the Arabian peninsula, possibly eastern Arabia or Yemen. Also appears in the Table of Nations (Gen 10:7, 10:29). Some scholars associate with the Hawlan region of Yemen.

locations.name: Cush (Eden context)
locations.notes: Region compassed by the Gihon river. In the Eden context, this may refer to a Mesopotamian Cush (Kassite territory) rather than the better-known African Cush (Nubia/Ethiopia). Identification uncertain and depends on Gihon identification.

locations.name: Land of Nod
locations.notes: Said to be 'east of Eden.' The Hebrew word 'nod' means 'wandering,' suggesting it may be symbolic rather than a real geographic location. No archaeological or geographic identification exists.

locations.name: Mountains of Ararat
locations.notes: The text says 'mountains of Ararat' (plural), referring to the region of ancient Urartu in eastern Anatolia/Armenia. Traditional identification with Mount Ararat (Agri Dagi, 5,137 m) in eastern Turkey dates to medieval period. The biblical text refers to the mountainous region, not a specific peak.

locations.name: Babel
locations.notes: Well-identified archaeological site of Babylon, ~85 km south of Baghdad, Iraq. Excavated extensively by Koldewey (1899-1917) and others. Site of the Tower of Babel narrative. Hebrew 'Babel' from Akkadian 'Bab-ilu' (Gate of God).

locations.name: Erech
locations.notes: Well-identified as Uruk (modern Warka), one of the earliest great cities in southern Mesopotamia. Extensive archaeological excavation since 1912. Associated with Gilgamesh. Part of Nimrod's kingdom.

locations.name: Akkad
locations.notes: Capital of the Akkadian Empire (c. 2334-2154 BCE). Despite its historical importance, the exact site has never been identified archaeologically. Location somewhere in central Mesopotamia, possibly near Sippar or Baghdad. Coordinates are approximate scholarly estimate.

locations.name: Calneh
locations.notes: Identification disputed. Traditionally placed in Babylonia. Some scholars identify with Kullani (Kunulua) in northern Syria (Tell Tayinat). Others emend the Hebrew text to read 'all of them' (kullana) rather than as a place name. Coordinates given for the Syrian identification.

locations.name: Shinar
locations.notes: Biblical name for southern Mesopotamia, broadly equivalent to Sumer and Akkad (Babylonia). Possibly related to Egyptian 'Sangar' or Hittite 'Shankhara'. Coordinates represent approximate center of the alluvial plain.

locations.name: Nineveh
locations.notes: Well-identified archaeological site across the Tigris from modern Mosul, Iraq. Capital of the Neo-Assyrian Empire. Excavated by Layard, Botta, and many subsequent expeditions. Mounds of Kuyunjik and Nebi Yunus.

locations.name: Rehoboth-Ir
locations.notes: City in Assyria mentioned alongside Nineveh. Some scholars interpret the Hebrew 'rehovot ir' as 'broad places of the city' (describing Nineveh's plazas) rather than a separate city. No certain identification.

locations.name: Calah
locations.notes: Well-identified as Nimrud (ancient Kalhu), ~30 km south of Mosul, Iraq. Major Assyrian capital excavated by Layard (1845) and Mallowan (1949-63). Spectacular finds include the Northwest Palace reliefs.

locations.name: Resen
locations.notes: Said to be between Nineveh and Calah. Possibly identified with Selamiyeh or Karamles between Kuyunjik and Nimrud, or with the site at modern Bash Tapia. No certain archaeological identification.

locations.name: Sidon
locations.notes: Well-identified as modern Saida, Lebanon. Major Phoenician port city. Extensive archaeological remains. Called the 'firstborn' of Canaan in the Table of Nations.

locations.name: Canaan
locations.notes: Well-attested region in ancient Near Eastern texts (Amarna letters: 'Kinahhu/Kinahna'). Broadly corresponds to the Levantine coastal region including modern Israel, Palestine, Lebanon, and western Syria. A central geographic concept throughout Genesis.

locations.name: Sodom
locations.notes: One of the Cities of the Plain. Location debated. Major candidates: Bab edh-Dhra (southeast of Dead Sea, Early Bronze Age site), Tall el-Hammam (northeast of Dead Sea, favored by some recent excavators), and the submerged southern Dead Sea theory. Coordinates given for Bab edh-Dhra, the most widely cited scholarly identification (Rast and Schaub excavations).

locations.name: Gomorrah
locations.notes: One of the Cities of the Plain, usually paired with Sodom. If Sodom is at Bab edh-Dhra, Gomorrah is often identified with Numeira, ~13 km to the south, also an Early Bronze Age site with destruction evidence (Rast and Schaub).

locations.name: Admah
locations.notes: One of the Cities of the Plain. No certain identification. If the southeastern Dead Sea location model is followed, possibly one of the unexcavated Early Bronze Age sites in the region such as es-Safi area.

locations.name: Zeboiim
locations.notes: One of the Cities of the Plain. No certain identification. Like Admah, identification depends on the model used for locating the pentapolis. Coordinates approximate based on the southeastern Dead Sea model.

locations.name: Zoar
locations.notes: Smallest of the five Cities of the Plain, spared from destruction at Lot's request. Usually identified with the area of es-Safi (Ghor es-Safi) at the southeastern end of the Dead Sea. The Madaba Map places Zoar here. Called Bela in Gen 14:2.

locations.name: Ur of the Chaldees
locations.notes: Most commonly identified with Tell el-Muqayyar in southern Iraq, excavated by Woolley (1922-1934). Famous for the Royal Tombs and ziggurat. Some scholars (Cyrus Gordon, et al.) proposed an alternate northern Ur near Harran, but the southern identification is standard.

locations.name: Haran
locations.notes: Well-identified as Harran in southeastern Turkey. Major trading center on the Balikh River tributary of the Euphrates. Known for its temple to the moon-god Sin. Mentioned in Mari and Nuzi texts. Abraham's father Terah settled here.

locations.name: Shechem
locations.notes: Well-identified as Tell Balata near modern Nablus. Excavated by Sellin, Wright, and others. Major Canaanite and Israelite city in the pass between Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim. First place Abraham stops in Canaan.

locations.name: Oak of Moreh
locations.notes: Sacred tree near Shechem where Abraham built an altar. The 'oak' or 'terebinth' (Hebrew 'elon') of Moreh. Located in or near Shechem. Some identify with the area of Tell Balata.

locations.name: Bethel
locations.notes: Identified with Beitin (traditional) or nearby el-Bireh (Livingston's proposal). Beitin identification by Edward Robinson (1838) is standard. Excavated by Albright and Kelso. Originally called Luz, renamed by Jacob. Key patriarchal sanctuary.

locations.name: Ai
locations.notes: Usually identified with et-Tell, east of Beitin/Bethel. Excavated by Marquet-Krause and Callaway. The Hebrew name means 'the ruin.' Problem: et-Tell shows no Late Bronze Age occupation. Some propose Khirbet el-Maqatir as alternative.

locations.name: Negev
locations.notes: Well-known arid region in southern Israel/Palestine. Hebrew 'negev' means 'dry' or 'south.' The area south of the Judean hill country extending toward the Sinai. Abraham traveled through it frequently.

locations.name: Egypt
locations.notes: Well-identified country along the Nile. Hebrew 'Mitsrayim' (dual form, possibly referring to Upper and Lower Egypt). Central to Genesis narratives of Abraham, Joseph, and Jacob. Coordinates are for Cairo/Memphis area.

locations.name: Nile River
locations.notes: Well-identified. Major river of Egypt. Hebrew 'ye'or' (from Egyptian 'itrw'). Central to Joseph's dream interpretation (seven years of plenty/famine). Coordinates at the Delta near Cairo.

locations.name: Hebron
locations.notes: Well-identified as modern al-Khalil (Hebron). One of the oldest continuously inhabited cities. The Cave of Machpelah (burial site of the patriarchs) is located here. Called Kirjath-arba in earlier references.

locations.name: Mamre
locations.notes: Usually identified with Ramet el-Khalil, ~3 km north of Hebron. Site of Herodian enclosure and earlier remains. Abraham's primary residence in Canaan, where he received the three visitors (Gen 18).

locations.name: Cave of Machpelah
locations.notes: Traditional location beneath the Herodian/Mamluk structure in central Hebron (Haram al-Ibrahimi). Purchased by Abraham as a burial cave from Ephron the Hittite. Burial place of Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah.

locations.name: Dan
locations.notes: Well-identified as Tell el-Qadi (Tell Dan) at the foot of Mount Hermon. Excavated by Biran (1966-1999). Originally called Laish. Abraham pursued the kings as far as Dan in Gen 14:14 (possibly an anachronistic name).

locations.name: Hobah
locations.notes: Said to be 'north of Damascus' where Abraham pursued the coalition of kings. Exact identification uncertain. Some associate with Hoba/Hobah ~60 km north of Damascus, or Apum mentioned in cuneiform texts.

locations.name: Damascus
locations.notes: Well-identified as modern Damascus, Syria. One of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world. Mentioned in connection with Abraham's servant Eliezer and the pursuit of the kings.

locations.name: Valley of Shaveh
locations.notes: Where Abraham met the king of Sodom after defeating the kings. Traditionally identified with the Kidron Valley near Jerusalem. Also called the 'King's Valley.' Josephus places it near Jerusalem.

locations.name: Salem
locations.notes: City of Melchizedek. Traditionally identified with Jerusalem (cf. Ps 76:2). The identification is ancient (Josephus, Targums) and widely accepted. Some scholars propose Salim near Shechem as alternative. Coordinates given for Jerusalem.

locations.name: River of Egypt
locations.notes: Southern boundary of the promised land. Usually identified with Wadi el-Arish in northern Sinai, not the Nile. Some scholars identify with Wadi Besor. This is the seasonal watercourse that marked the traditional border between Canaan and Egypt.

locations.name: Beer-lahai-roi
aliases: Well of the Living One Who Sees Me
locations.notes: Well where Hagar encountered the angel. Said to be between Kadesh and Bered. Exact location unknown. Various proposals in the northern Negev/southern Judean desert. Coordinates approximate based on placement between Kadesh-barnea and the Negev.

locations.name: Kadesh
locations.notes: Usually identified with Tell el-Qudeirat or nearby Ain el-Qudeirat in the northeastern Sinai. Important oasis and way-station. Called En-mishpat ('Spring of Judgment') in Gen 14:7. Excavated by Dothan and Cohen.

locations.name: Shur
locations.notes: Desert region east of Egypt, on the way to Assyria (or Shur = 'wall,' possibly referring to Egyptian frontier fortifications). Located in the northern Sinai. Hagar was found near a spring on the road to Shur.

locations.name: Gerar
locations.notes: City of Abimelech. Identification debated between Tell Abu Hureirah (Tell Haror), excavated by Oren, and Tell Jemmeh. Tell Haror is the more widely accepted identification in current scholarship. Located in the western Negev.

locations.name: Beer-sheba
locations.notes: Well-identified as Tell es-Seba (archaeological site) and modern Be'er Sheva. Excavated by Aharoni. The name means either 'Well of the Oath' or 'Well of Seven.' Abraham and Isaac both made covenants here. Major patriarchal site.

locations.name: Mount Moriah
locations.notes: Site of the Binding of Isaac (Aqedah). Traditionally identified with the Temple Mount in Jerusalem (2 Chr 3:1). Some scholars question this identification. The Samaritan tradition places it at Mount Gerizim. Coordinates given for the Temple Mount.

locations.name: Aram-Naharaim
locations.notes: Literally 'Aram of the two rivers,' referring to the region of upper Mesopotamia between the Euphrates and the Habur (or Balikh) rivers. Corresponds roughly to the Mitanni region. Where Abraham's servant went to find Rebekah.

locations.name: City of Nahor
locations.notes: Destination of Abraham's servant seeking a wife for Isaac. Usually identified with or near Haran, or with Nakhur mentioned in Mari texts (in the upper Habur region). Coordinates approximate, near Haran.

locations.name: Paddan-aram
locations.notes: Region around Haran in upper Mesopotamia. The name may mean 'field/plain of Aram.' Likely corresponds to the agricultural plain around Harran. Where Jacob went to find a wife from Laban's household.

locations.name: Edom
locations.notes: Well-identified mountainous region southeast of the Dead Sea, in modern southern Jordan. Associated with Esau. Also called Seir (Mount Seir). Archaeological surveys by Levy and others have documented extensive habitation.

locations.name: Mount Seir
locations.notes: Mountainous region of Edom, east of the Arabah. The name refers to the entire mountain range in southern Jordan. Originally inhabited by Horites before the Edomites. Esau settled here.

locations.name: Jabbok River
locations.notes: Well-identified as modern Wadi Zarqa in Jordan. Major tributary of the Jordan River from the east. Site of Jacob's wrestling with the angel. Flows ~65 km from near Amman to the Jordan Valley.

locations.name: Peniel
locations.notes: Where Jacob wrestled with the angel and was renamed Israel. The name means 'Face of God.' Usually located near the Jabbok crossing, possibly at Tulul edh-Dhahab (two mounds on the Jabbok). Exact identification debated.

locations.name: Mahanaim
locations.notes: Where Jacob saw angels and named the place 'Two Camps.' Location debated. Proposals include Tell edh-Dhahab el-Gharbi, Tell Hajjaj, and Tulul edh-Dhahab. Located in the Transjordanian hills, north of the Jabbok.

locations.name: Succoth (Transjordan)
locations.notes: City in the Jordan Valley east of the Jordan River. Usually identified with Tell Deir Alla in the Jordan Valley of modern Jordan. Excavated by Franken. Jacob built booths here for his livestock. Name means 'booths/shelters.'

locations.name: Bethlehem
locations.notes: Well-identified as modern Bethlehem, ~8 km south of Jerusalem. Called Ephrath/Ephrathah in Genesis. Rachel died and was buried on the way to Ephrath. Extensive archaeological remains from Bronze Age onward.

locations.name: Migdal Eder
locations.notes: Literally 'Tower of the Flock.' Located beyond Ephrath where Israel camped. Exact location unknown but traditionally placed between Bethlehem and Hebron. Jerome identified it ~1 mile from Bethlehem.

locations.name: Dothan
locations.notes: Well-identified as Tell Dothan, ~22 km north of Shechem. Excavated by Free (1953-1964). Significant Middle and Late Bronze Age remains. Where Joseph found his brothers pasturing flocks.

locations.name: Gilead
locations.notes: Well-known mountainous region east of the Jordan River in modern Jordan. Extends from the Arnon River northward. Laban overtook Jacob in the hill country of Gilead. Rich in forests and pasturage.

locations.name: Galeed
locations.notes: Heap of stones set up by Jacob and Laban as a witness/boundary marker in Gilead. Hebrew 'Galeed' = 'heap of witness'; Aramaic 'Jegar-sahadutha' = same meaning. Also called Mizpah ('watchtower'). Exact location unknown within Gilead.

locations.name: Goshen
locations.notes: Region in the eastern Nile Delta where Jacob's family settled. Usually identified with Wadi Tumilat region. Also called 'land of Rameses.' The area around Tell el-Maskhuta and Tell el-Retaba. Fertile pastureland suitable for herders.

locations.name: On
locations.notes: Well-identified as ancient Heliopolis (Iunu), now a suburb of Cairo (Ain Shams/Matariya). Major Egyptian religious center, cult center of the sun-god Ra. Potiphera, priest of On, was Joseph's father-in-law.

locations.name: Jordan River
locations.notes: Well-identified major river flowing from the Sea of Galilee to the Dead Sea. ~251 km long. The Jordan Valley (Kikkar) is prominently mentioned as the well-watered plain Lot chose.

locations.name: Salt Sea
locations.notes: Well-identified body of water. The Vale of Siddim, site of the battle in Gen 14, is said to be 'full of bitumen pits' and identical with the Salt Sea. Lowest point on earth's surface.

locations.name: El-paran
locations.notes: Mentioned in the campaign of the four kings (Gen 14:6). Said to be 'by the wilderness.' Possibly related to Eilat/Elath at the head of the Gulf of Aqaba, or a location in the Arabah wilderness.

locations.name: Hazazon-tamar
locations.notes: Mentioned in connection with the Amorites defeated by the four kings. Identified with En-gedi by 2 Chr 20:2. Tell el-Jurn near En-gedi on the western shore of the Dead Sea. Palm trees noted in the name (tamar).

locations.name: Valley of Siddim
locations.notes: Battlefield of the five kings vs. four kings, said to be the Salt Sea. Usually placed at the southern end of the Dead Sea, possibly now submerged. Full of bitumen pits. The exact area is debated.

locations.name: Rehoboth (Well)
locations.notes: Well dug by Isaac's servants meaning 'broad places' or 'room.' One of a series of wells in the Gerar area (along with Esek and Sitnah). Possibly Ruheibeh in the western Negev, ~30 km southwest of Beer-sheba.

locations.name: Esek
locations.notes: Well dug by Isaac's servants near Gerar, meaning 'contention.' Disputed with the herdsmen of Gerar. No certain identification. Located in the Wadi Gerar (Nahal Gerar) area.

locations.name: Sitnah
locations.notes: Second well dug by Isaac's servants near Gerar, meaning 'enmity' or 'accusation.' Also disputed. No certain identification. Located between Esek and Rehoboth in the Gerar area.

locations.name: El-bethel
locations.notes: Altar built by Jacob at Bethel, named 'God of Bethel.' Same general location as Bethel. The name commemorates God's appearance to Jacob when he fled from Esau.

locations.name: Allon-bacuth
locations.notes: Oak tree below Bethel where Deborah (Rebekah's nurse) was buried. The name means 'Oak of Weeping.' Located below/near Bethel. No specific identification beyond the Bethel vicinity.

locations.name: Rachel's Tomb
locations.notes: Said to be on the road to Ephrath/Bethlehem. Traditional site just north of Bethlehem on the road from Jerusalem. However, 1 Sam 10:2 places it in Benjamin territory, leading some scholars to locate it further north near Ramah.

locations.name: Abel-mizraim
locations.notes: Threshing floor of Atad, 'beyond the Jordan,' where the funeral procession for Jacob mourned. The Canaanites named it Abel-mizraim ('mourning of Egypt'). Location uncertain. The 'beyond the Jordan' places it in Transjordan.

locations.name: Land of the Philistines
locations.notes: Coastal region of southwestern Canaan, later associated with the Philistine pentapolis. Genesis references are considered anachronistic by most scholars, as the Philistines arrived c. 1175 BCE. Abimelech of Gerar is called 'king of the Philistines.'

locations.name: Elam
locations.notes: Ancient kingdom in southwestern Iran, capital at Susa. Well-attested in Mesopotamian texts and archaeology. Chedorlaomer, king of Elam, led the coalition in Gen 14. Also in the Table of Nations as a son of Shem.

locations.name: Asshur
locations.notes: Well-identified. The city of Ashur (Qal'at Sherqat) on the Tigris was the original capital. Assyria as a region/empire is well-attested. In Table of Nations, a son of Shem. Coordinates for the city of Ashur.

locations.name: Arphaxad
locations.notes: Son of Shem in Table of Nations and ancestor of Abraham. Possibly connected to a region in upper Mesopotamia. Josephus associated it with Chaldea. Identification with a specific geographic area remains uncertain.

locations.name: Lud
locations.notes: Son of Shem in Table of Nations. Usually identified with Lydia in western Anatolia (Turkey). Some scholars associate with Lubdu in Mesopotamia. Coordinates for Lydian capital Sardis area.

locations.name: Aram
locations.notes: Son of Shem in Table of Nations. Well-identified region and people centered on Syria/upper Mesopotamia. The Arameans became prominent from the late 2nd millennium BCE. Damascus was a major Aramean center.

locations.name: Cush (Africa)
locations.notes: Son of Ham in Table of Nations. Well-identified as the region south of Egypt, ancient Nubia (modern Sudan/southern Egypt). The Kingdom of Kush had its capital at Kerma, then Napata, then Meroe.

locations.name: Put
locations.notes: Son of Ham in Table of Nations. Usually identified with Libya (ancient Put/Phut mentioned in Egyptian texts). Some identify with Punt (Horn of Africa). Coordinates for Libya/Cyrenaica coast.

locations.name: Seba
locations.notes: Son of Cush in Table of Nations. Usually identified with a region in upper Nubia/Sudan, or with Meroe. Distinguished from Sheba (son of Joktan). Possibly connected to the Sabaeans of the Ethiopian/Sudanese region.

locations.name: Raamah
locations.notes: Son of Cush in Table of Nations. Usually identified with a region in southwestern Arabia, possibly Najran area or southeastern Arabia. The name appears in Sabaean inscriptions.

locations.name: Sheba
locations.notes: Son of Raamah (Gen 10:7) and also son of Joktan (Gen 10:28). Identified with the Sabaean kingdom in southwestern Arabia (modern Yemen). Capital at Marib. Famous for the Queen of Sheba. Well-attested in inscriptions.

locations.name: Dedan
locations.notes: Son of Raamah (Gen 10:7) and also son of Jokshan (Gen 25:3). Identified with al-Ula (ancient Dedan) in northwestern Arabia (Hejaz). Extensive inscriptions and archaeological remains. Major oasis and trading center.

locations.name: Nimrod's Kingdom
locations.notes: Not a specific location but the domain of Nimrod, 'a mighty hunter.' Included Babel, Erech, Akkad, and Calneh in Shinar, then expanded to Assyria. Nimrod may reflect Naram-Sin or Tukulti-Ninurta traditions.

locations.name: Ludim
locations.notes: Descendant of Mizraim in Table of Nations. Usually associated with a Libyan people. Distinguished from Lud (son of Shem). Possibly connected to the Libyans west of Egypt.

locations.name: Pathrusim
locations.notes: Descendant of Mizraim. Identified with Pathros, the Egyptian 'Pa-to-ris' meaning 'land of the south' = Upper Egypt (southern Egypt). Coordinates near Thebes/Luxor area.

locations.name: Caphtorim
locations.notes: Descendant of Mizraim. Usually identified with Crete (Egyptian 'Keftiu') or more broadly the Aegean region. The Philistines are said to come from Caphtor. Coordinates for central Crete.

locations.name: Heth
locations.notes: Son of Canaan in Table of Nations. The 'sons of Heth' in Genesis are the Hittites of Hebron from whom Abraham bought Machpelah. Their relationship to the Anatolian Hittite Empire is debated. Coordinates for Hebron where they are encountered.

locations.name: Jebusite Territory
locations.notes: Jebusites are descendants of Canaan (Gen 10:16). Their territory centered on Jerusalem (Jebus). Well-identified archaeologically with Bronze and Iron Age Jerusalem.

locations.name: Amorite Territory
locations.notes: Amorites are descendants of Canaan (Gen 10:16). In Genesis, they inhabit the hill country and Transjordan. Well-attested in Mesopotamian texts as 'Amurru.' Mamre, Eshcol, and Aner are called Amorites allied with Abraham.

locations.name: Gaza
locations.notes: Well-identified as modern Gaza. Ancient city on the southern Mediterranean coast. Mentioned as a boundary marker of Canaanite territory. One of the later Philistine pentapolis cities. Continuous occupation since Bronze Age.

locations.name: Lasha
locations.notes: Eastern boundary of Canaanite territory. Identification uncertain. Jerome identified it with Callirrhoe, hot springs on the east side of the Dead Sea. Others identify with Laish/Dan. Coordinates for Callirrhoe identification.

locations.name: Mesha
locations.notes: Boundary point of the Joktanite peoples (Gen 10:30), 'from Mesha as you go toward Sephar.' Identification unknown. Proposals include Massa in northern Arabia and various locations. Often associated with the western Arabian context.

locations.name: Sephar
locations.notes: Eastern boundary of Joktanite territory, 'hill country of the east.' Proposals include Dhofar in southern Oman and various Arabian locations. Identification remains speculative.

locations.name: Ophir
locations.notes: Son of Joktan in Table of Nations. Famous later as source of gold (1 Kings). Proposals include southwestern Arabia (Yemen/Oman), Somalia, India, and Zimbabwe. Most scholars favor a location in southwestern Arabia or the Horn of Africa.

locations.name: Plain of the Jordan
locations.notes: The well-watered plain ('kikkar') of the Jordan that Lot chose. Identified with the lower Jordan Valley, particularly the area around the Dead Sea's northern end. Described as being 'like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt.'

locations.name: Mountain East of Bethel
locations.notes: Abraham pitched his tent on the mountain east of Bethel with Bethel to the west and Ai to the east. This is the central hill country ridge. General location identifiable from Bethel and Ai positions.

locations.name: Bered
locations.notes: Mentioned as a reference point for Beer-lahai-roi, which is 'between Kadesh and Bered.' Possibly identified with Tell el-Khalasa (Elusa) in the northern Negev. Identification uncertain.

locations.name: Wilderness of Paran
locations.notes: Wilderness area in the northeastern Sinai peninsula. Ishmael settled in this wilderness. Located generally between Kadesh-barnea and the Gulf of Aqaba. The exact boundaries are uncertain.

locations.name: Ellasar
locations.notes: Kingdom of Arioch in Gen 14. Often identified with Larsa in southern Mesopotamia (modern Tell es-Senkereh). Some scholars connect it with Ilanzura in northern Syria. Larsa identification is more traditional. Excavated by Parrot and others.

locations.name: Goiim
locations.notes: Kingdom of Tidal 'king of Goiim' (nations). The Hebrew word simply means 'nations.' Tidal is sometimes connected to the Hittite name Tudhaliya. The geographic location of this kingdom is entirely uncertain.

locations.name: Rephaim Territory
locations.notes: The Rephaim were defeated at Ashteroth-karnaim. Ashtaroth is identified with Tell Ashtarah in the Bashan region of southern Syria/northern Jordan. Karnaim may be nearby Sheikh Sa'ad.

locations.name: Ham
locations.notes: Location where the Zuzim were defeated (Gen 14:5). Possibly identified with Ham in northern Gilead/Bashan. Some connect with modern Ham in Jordan. The Zuzim may be related to the Zamzummim.

locations.name: Shaveh-kiriathaim
locations.notes: Where the Emim were defeated (Gen 14:5). Kiriathaim is possibly Khirbet el-Qureiyat in Moab, east of the Dead Sea. The name means 'plain of the double city.'

locations.name: Amalekite Territory
locations.notes: The territory later associated with the Amalekites. Gen 14:7 mentions the 'country of the Amalekites' (likely anachronistic since Amalek descends from Esau in Gen 36:12). Located in the Negev/northern Sinai region.

locations.name: Timna
locations.notes: Edomite clan/region associated with Timna, concubine of Esau's son Eliphaz (Gen 36:12). May be associated with the Timna Valley copper mining area in the southern Arabah, ~30 km north of Eilat.

locations.name: Timnah (Judah)
locations.notes: City where Judah met Tamar. Usually identified with Tell Batash in the Sorek Valley. Excavated by Kelm and Mazar. Located on the border between Judean hill country and the Philistine plain.

locations.name: Enaim
locations.notes: Place on the road to Timnah where Tamar sat. Mentioned only in Gen 38:14, 21. The name may mean 'two springs' or 'open place.' Possibly Khirbet el-Aenab near Timnah. Exact identification uncertain.

locations.name: Chezib
locations.notes: Birthplace of Judah's son Shelah. Usually identified with Tell el-Beida (Achzib) in the Shephelah. May be the same as Achzib of Judah (Josh 15:44). Located in the lowlands southwest of Hebron.

locations.name: Rameses
locations.notes: Identified with Pi-Ramesses/Qantir in the eastern Nile Delta. Major Egyptian royal residence of Ramesses II. The 'land of Rameses' in Genesis is likely synonymous with Goshen. Extensive archaeological excavations.

locations.name: Kenite Territory
locations.notes: One of the peoples whose land was promised to Abraham (Gen 15:19). The Kenites are associated with the Negev and Sinai region, particularly the area south of Arad. Possibly connected with copper smelting (qayin = smith).

locations.name: Kenizzite Territory
locations.notes: One of the peoples whose land was promised to Abraham (Gen 15:19). Possibly related to Kenaz, an Edomite clan (Gen 36:11, 15, 42). Associated with the southern Negev and northern Edom area.

locations.name: Kadmonite Territory
locations.notes: One of the peoples whose land was promised to Abraham (Gen 15:19). The name means 'easterners.' Location uncertain. May refer to peoples east of the Jordan or in the Syrian desert.

locations.name: Hittite Territory
locations.notes: In the Genesis context, the Hittites are encountered at Hebron (sons of Heth). Their relationship to the Anatolian Hittite Empire (Hattusa, central Turkey) is debated. The Genesis Hittites may be a local Canaanite group.

locations.name: Perizzite Territory
locations.notes: One of the peoples in the land promised to Abraham (Gen 15:20). Mentioned alongside Canaanites in the central hill country. The name may mean 'village dwellers.' No specific territorial boundaries known.

locations.name: Girgashite Territory
locations.notes: One of the peoples in the land promised to Abraham (Gen 15:21). Mentioned also as descendants of Canaan (Gen 10:16). Possibly connected with 'Qarqisha' in Ugaritic texts. No certain geographic identification.

locations.name: Hivite Territory
locations.notes: Descendants of Canaan (Gen 10:17). In Genesis, the Hivites appear at Shechem (Hamor the Hivite, Gen 34). Some scholars emend to 'Horite.' Also associated with the Lebanon/Hermon region elsewhere in the Bible.

locations.name: Horite Territory
locations.notes: Pre-Edomite inhabitants of Mount Seir. The Horites of Seir are listed in Gen 36:20-30. Sometimes connected with the Hurrians of Mesopotamia, though this is debated. Territory in the mountains of Edom.

locations.name: Sinite Territory
locations.notes: Descendants of Canaan (Gen 10:17). Possibly associated with the city of Siannu/Siyanu on the Phoenician coast of Syria (near modern Jableh), or with the wilderness of Sin.

locations.name: Arvadite Territory
locations.notes: Descendants of Canaan (Gen 10:18). Well-identified as the island of Arwad (Ruad) off the coast of Syria, north of Tripoli. Important Phoenician city-state. Still inhabited today.

locations.name: Hamathite Territory
locations.notes: Descendants of Canaan (Gen 10:18). Well-identified as Hamath (modern Hama) on the Orontes River in Syria. Major city-state. Excavated by Danish expeditions. 'Lebo-Hamath' served as the northern boundary of Canaan.

locations.name: Zemarite Territory
locations.notes: Descendants of Canaan (Gen 10:18). Usually identified with Sumur/Simyra (Tell Kazel) on the coast of Syria. Mentioned in Egyptian Amarna letters and Assyrian texts. Important Phoenician/Syrian coastal city.

locations.name: Tower of Babel
locations.notes: Usually associated with the great ziggurat of Babylon (Etemenanki, 'House of the Foundation of Heaven and Earth'). The foundations were excavated by Koldewey. Some scholars also consider Eridu's ziggurat. Same coordinates as Babel/Babylon.

locations.name: Valley of Gerar
locations.notes: Valley/wadi near Gerar where Isaac settled and dug wells. Identified with Nahal Gerar (Wadi esh-Sheri'ah) in the western Negev. Isaac dug the wells of Esek, Sitnah, and Rehoboth in this area.

locations.name: Shibah
locations.notes: Well at Beer-sheba named by Isaac on the day of his covenant with Abimelech. The name 'Shibah' relates to 'oath' (shevuah) or 'seven.' Same location as Beer-sheba.

locations.name: Wilderness of Beer-sheba
locations.notes: Arid area around Beer-sheba where Hagar wandered after being sent away with Ishmael. Part of the northern Negev desert.

locations.name: Eshcol
locations.notes: Eshcol appears as an Amorite ally of Abraham (Gen 14:13, 24). Also the name of a valley near Hebron (Num 13:23-24), meaning 'cluster' (of grapes). The person and place may be connected. Located near Hebron.

locations.name: Mediterranean Sea
locations.notes: Not directly named in Genesis but implied as the western boundary of Canaan. The Mediterranean coast defines the western edge of the territory of Canaan described in Gen 10:19.

locations.name: Bozrah
locations.notes: Capital of Edom mentioned in the Edomite king list. Identified with Buseirah in southern Jordan. Excavated by Bennett (1971-1980). Significant Iron Age fortified city. City of Jobab son of Zerah (Gen 36:33).

locations.name: Dinhabah
locations.notes: City of Bela son of Beor, first Edomite king (Gen 36:32). Location unknown. No certain identification.

locations.name: Teman
locations.notes: Edomite clan/district, possibly the name of a specific settlement. Usually located in the northern part of Edom. Sometimes identified with Tawilan near Petra. Grandson of Esau (Gen 36:11, 15).

locations.name: Avith
locations.notes: City of Hadad son of Bedad, Edomite king (Gen 36:35). Location unknown.

locations.name: Field of Moab
locations.notes: Region east of the Dead Sea. Hadad son of Bedad defeated Midian 'in the field of Moab' (Gen 36:35). Moab is well-identified as the plateau east of the Dead Sea in modern Jordan.

locations.name: Masrekah
locations.notes: City of Samlah, Edomite king (Gen 36:36). Location unknown. The name may relate to 'vineyard.' No certain identification.

locations.name: Rehoboth-on-the-River
locations.notes: City of Shaul, Edomite king (Gen 36:37). 'The River' usually means the Euphrates, but an Edomite connection to the Euphrates is unexpected. Some suggest a local wadi. No certain identification.

locations.name: Pau
locations.notes: City of Hadar/Hadad, Edomite king (Gen 36:39). Location unknown. No certain identification.

locations.name: Moab (origin)
locations.notes: Moab is born to Lot's elder daughter (Gen 19:37). Ancestor of the Moabites, who occupied the plateau east of the Dead Sea. Well-attested in archaeology and the Mesha Stele.

locations.name: Ammon (origin)
locations.notes: Ben-ammi (Ammon) is born to Lot's younger daughter (Gen 19:38). Ancestor of the Ammonites. Their capital Rabbath-ammon is modern Amman, Jordan. Well-attested in archaeology.

locations.name: Midian
locations.notes: Son of Abraham by Keturah (Gen 25:2). The Midianites occupied the northwestern Arabian peninsula, east of the Gulf of Aqaba. Joseph was sold to Midianite traders. Possibly centered near modern al-Bad' in Saudi Arabia.

locations.name: Ishmael's Territory
locations.notes: Ishmael's descendants 'settled from Havilah to Shur, which is east of Egypt in the direction of Assyria' (Gen 25:18). This describes the northern Arabian desert. Ishmaelite traders appear in the Joseph story.

locations.name: Joktanite Territory
locations.notes: Joktan's 13 sons (Gen 10:26-29) are generally identified with Arabian tribal regions, mostly in southern Arabia (Yemen/Oman). The territory extended 'from Mesha in the direction of Sephar, the hill country of the east' (Gen 10:30).

locations.name: Hazarmaveth
locations.notes: Son of Joktan (Gen 10:26). Well-identified as Hadramawt (Hadhramaut), a major region in eastern Yemen/southern Arabia. The identification is supported by the close phonetic correspondence and is widely accepted.

locations.name: Uzal
locations.notes: Son of Joktan (Gen 10:27). Traditionally identified with Sana'a (Azal), the capital of Yemen. The ancient name of Sana'a was reportedly Azal. This identification is widely accepted though not archaeologically proven.

locations.name: Gomer
locations.notes: Son of Japheth (Gen 10:2). Usually identified with the Cimmerians (Akkadian 'Gimirri'), who invaded Anatolia from the north in the 8th-7th centuries BCE. Associated with the region north of the Black Sea and later Anatolia.

locations.name: Magog
locations.notes: Son of Japheth (Gen 10:2). Often associated with the Scythians (Josephus) or peoples of the Caucasus region. Later apocalyptic literature (Ezekiel 38-39, Revelation) uses Gog and Magog symbolically. Exact identification uncertain.

locations.name: Madai
locations.notes: Son of Japheth (Gen 10:2). Well-identified with the Medes of western Iran. The Median Empire (678-549 BCE) had its capital at Ecbatana (modern Hamadan). Well-attested in Assyrian and Persian sources.

locations.name: Javan
locations.notes: Son of Japheth (Gen 10:2). Well-identified with the Ionians/Greeks. Hebrew 'Yavan' corresponds to Greek 'Ionia' (western Anatolia). The name appears in Assyrian texts as 'Yamanaya.' Coordinates for Ionia region.

locations.name: Tubal
locations.notes: Son of Japheth (Gen 10:2). Usually identified with Tabal, a Neo-Hittite/Luwian kingdom in central Anatolia (southeastern Turkey). Mentioned in Assyrian texts alongside Muski (Meshech).

locations.name: Meshech
locations.notes: Son of Japheth (Gen 10:2). Usually identified with the Muski/Mushki of Assyrian texts, located in eastern Anatolia. Often paired with Tubal. Later confused with Moscow by medieval traditions, but this is incorrect.

locations.name: Tiras
locations.notes: Son of Japheth (Gen 10:2). Possibly identified with the Tyrrhenians (Etruscans) or the Tursha of the Sea Peoples. Some associate with Thrace. Identification remains debated.

locations.name: Ashkenaz
locations.notes: Son of Gomer (Gen 10:3). Usually identified with the Scythians (Assyrian 'Ashkuza/Ishkuza'), who were active in the Caucasus and Anatolia in the 7th century BCE. Later the term was applied to Germany/Central Europe in Jewish tradition.

locations.name: Riphath
locations.notes: Son of Gomer (Gen 10:3). Identification uncertain. Sometimes connected with the Paphlagonians of northern Anatolia. Josephus associated them with the Paphlagonians.

locations.name: Togarmah
locations.notes: Son of Gomer (Gen 10:3). Identified with Tegarama/Til-garimmu in eastern Anatolia, known from Hittite and Assyrian texts. A region famous for horses and mules (Ezek 27:14). Modern Gurun area in Turkey.

locations.name: Elishah
locations.notes: Son of Javan (Gen 10:4). Often identified with Alashiya, which most scholars equate with Cyprus or part of Cyprus. Mentioned in Amarna letters and other ancient texts. Some propose identification with Elis in Greece.

locations.name: Tarshish
locations.notes: Son of Javan (Gen 10:4). Most commonly identified with Tartessus in southwestern Spain. Other proposals include Tarsus in Cilicia and Sardinia. Famous as a source of metals. The westernmost location in the Table of Nations.

locations.name: Kittim
locations.notes: Son of Javan (Gen 10:4). Identified with Kition (modern Larnaca) on Cyprus. The name was later extended to refer to Cyprus generally and then to western maritime peoples including Romans in Daniel and Dead Sea Scrolls.

locations.name: Dodanim
locations.notes: Son of Javan (Gen 10:4). The parallel in 1 Chr 1:7 reads 'Rodanim,' suggesting Rhodes. The textual variant (d/r confusion in Hebrew) makes the identification uncertain. Coordinates for Rhodes.

locations.name: Uz
locations.notes: Son of Aram (Gen 10:23). Later, the land of Uz is the home of Job. Usually located in the Transjordanian region, possibly Edom/Hauran area. Also appears as son of Nahor (Gen 22:21). Exact location debated.

locations.name: Mountain (Lot's refuge)
locations.notes: After fleeing Zoar, Lot and his daughters went up to a mountain and lived in a cave. Location unspecified beyond being in the hills above/near Zoar. Traditionally placed in the mountains southeast of the Dead Sea.

locations.name: Sabtah
locations.notes: Son of Cush in Table of Nations. Possibly identified with Shabwat (capital of Hadramawt) or with a location on the eastern Arabian coast. Identification uncertain.

locations.name: Sabteca
locations.notes: Son of Cush in Table of Nations. Identification unknown. Sometimes placed in eastern Arabia or the Horn of Africa. No scholarly consensus.
